input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

input.initialPrompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command: dotnet --version 2>/dev/null && dotnet new console -n PersonJson -o PersonJson --framework net11.0 2>&1
arguments.description: Check dotnet version and create console project

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet version and create console project

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using the built-in `System.Text.Json` serializer. They want to use whatever the framework provides out of the box.

In .NET 11 (which would be .NET 9's successor - actually .NET 11 would be released in 2026), `System.Text.Json` has a `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` built-in naming policy. This was actually introduced in .NET 9 (I need to verify this). Let me think...

Actually, looking at the history:


- .NET 7 added `JsonNamingPolicy.SnakeCaseLower` and `JsonNamingPolicy.SnakeCaseUpper` and `JsonNamingPolicy.KebabCaseLower` and `JsonNamingPolicy.KebabCaseUpper`
- `JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase` has been there since the beginning
- `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` - I'm not 100% sure when this was added, but it's plausible it exists in .NET 11

Actually, wait. By default, `System.Text.Json` serializes properties using their exact names as defined in the class, so I need to check what naming policies are actually available in .NET 11 and whether PascalCase is one of them. their original C# names. So if the record is `Person(string name, int age)`, the parameters are lowercase, but the generated properties would actually be `name` and `age` (since the record parameter names become property names with the same casing... wait, no).

Actually, in C# records, `public record Person(string name, int age)` generates properties `name` and `age` (matching the parameter names exactly). So to get PascalCase output, we need a naming policy.

Wait, let me reconsider. In C#, `public record Person(string name, int age)` - the positional parameters generate public properties. The property names match the parameter names. So `name` and `age` would be the property names (lowercase). 

Actually, I need to be more careful. In C# records, the synthesized properties use the exact casing of the parameters. So `string name` becomes a property `name` (lowercase) and `int age` becomes property `age` (lowercase).

So to serialize with PascalCase property names in JSON, I'd need a naming policy. But I'm realizing `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` might not actually exist in .NET. Looking at what's available in recent versions, there's `CamelCase`, `SnakeCaseLower`, `SnakeCaseUpper`, `KebabCaseLower`, and `KebabCaseUpper`, but no built-in PascalCase option since the default already preserves the original casing.

Given the user is on .NET 11 and wants out-of-the-box solutions, I should check if that version added `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase`. If not, the simplest fix would be to just rename the record parameters to PascalCase in the first place.

Let me create a console project targeting net11.0 and test this approach.
